---
title: "Lesson 1:  The Cramér-Rao Lower Bound"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_fisher-information-and-the-cramer-rao-lower-bound.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_mean-squared-error-bias-and-relative-efficiency.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-fisher-information-and-the-cramer-rao-lower-bound_module3_lesson1.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## slides on The Cramér-Rao Lower Bound {#sec-cramer-rao-lower-bound}

Let $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n$ be a random sample from a distribution with joint density or probability mass function $f(\mathbf{x};\theta)$.

Suppose that the target is the parameter function $\tau(\theta)$ and that

$$
T=t(\mathbf{X})
$$

is an unbiased estimator of $\tau(\theta)$, so that

$$
\mathbb{E}_\theta[T]=\tau(\theta)
$$

::: {#thm-cramer-rao-lower-bound}
## Cramér-Rao Lower Bound

Under the regularity conditions given below, every unbiased estimator $T$ of $\tau(\theta)$ satisfies

$$
\operatorname{Var}_\theta(T)
\geq
\frac{[\tau'(\theta)]^2}{I_n(\theta)}
$$
:::

::: {#def-fisher-information}
## Fisher Information

The Fisher information in the full sample $\mathbf{X}=(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$ is

$$
I_n(\theta)
:=
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right)^2
\right]
$$
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Notation

Writing an unbiased estimator as

$$
\widehat{\tau(\theta)}=T=t(\mathbf{X})
$$

the Cramér-Rao lower bound can be written as

$$
\operatorname{Var}_\theta(T)
\geq
\frac{[\tau'(\theta)]^2}{I_n(\theta)}
$$
:::

## The Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality {#sec-cauchy-schwarz}

The proof of the Cramér-Rao lower bound depends on the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.

::: {#thm-cauchy-schwarz-integral}
## Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality

For square-integrable functions $g$ and $h$,

$$
\left(
\int g(x)h(x)\,dx
\right)^2
\leq
\left(
\int g^2(x)\,dx
\right)
\left(
\int h^2(x)\,dx
\right)
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Consider the nonnegative integral

$$
\iint
\bigl[g(x)h(y)-g(y)h(x)\bigr]^2
\,dx\,dy
\geq 0
$$

Expanding the square gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
0
&\leq
\iint g^2(x)h^2(y)\,dx\,dy \\
&\quad
-2\iint g(x)h(y)g(y)h(x)\,dx\,dy \\
&\quad
+\iint g^2(y)h^2(x)\,dx\,dy
\end{aligned}
$$

The first and third terms are equal, so

$$
\begin{aligned}
0
&\leq
2\iint g^2(x)h^2(y)\,dx\,dy
-2\iint g(x)h(y)g(y)h(x)\,dx\,dy \\
&=
2\left(
\int g^2(x)\,dx
\right)
\left(
\int h^2(y)\,dy
\right)
-2\left(
\int g(x)h(x)\,dx
\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging yields

$$
\left(
\int g(x)h(x)\,dx
\right)^2
\leq
\left(
\int g^2(x)\,dx
\right)
\left(
\int h^2(x)\,dx
\right)
$$
:::

The same inequality holds when the integrals are replaced by sums. It also holds with $dx$ replaced by $f(x)\,dx$, where $f$ is a probability density function:

$$
\left(
\mathbb{E}[g(X)h(X)]
\right)^2
\leq
\mathbb{E}[g^2(X)]\,
\mathbb{E}[h^2(X)]
$$

## Proof of the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound {#sec-proof-cramer-rao}

To apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, define

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(\mathbf{X})
&=T-\tau(\theta) \\
h(\mathbf{X})
&=
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

It is enough to establish the identity

$$
\tau'(\theta)
=
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[
\bigl(T-\tau(\theta)\bigr)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
$$

Once this identity is established,

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\tau'(\theta)]^2
&=
\left(
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[
\bigl(T-\tau(\theta)\bigr)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
\right)^2 \\
&\leq
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[
\bigl(T-\tau(\theta)\bigr)^2
\right]
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right)^2
\right]
&& \text{Cauchy-Schwarz} \\
&=
\operatorname{Var}_\theta(T)\,I_n(\theta)
&& \text{unbiasedness and Fisher information}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\boxed{
\operatorname{Var}_\theta(T)
\geq
\frac{[\tau'(\theta)]^2}{I_n(\theta)}
}
$$

### Deriving the key identity {#sec-crlb-key-identity}

Since $T$ is unbiased,

$$
\tau(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_\theta[T]
$$

Differentiating gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau'(\theta)
&=
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\mathbb{E}_\theta[T] \\
&=
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\int t(\mathbf{x})f(\mathbf{x};\theta)\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&=
\int t(\mathbf{x})
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x}
&& \text{differentiate under the integral} \\
&=
\int
\bigl(t(\mathbf{x})-\tau(\theta)\bigr)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&\quad
+
\tau(\theta)
\underbrace{
\int
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x}
}_{
\displaystyle
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\int f(\mathbf{x};\theta)\,d\mathbf{x}
=
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}1
=0
} \\
&=
\int
\bigl(t(\mathbf{x})-\tau(\theta)\bigr)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
=
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau'(\theta)
&=
\int
\bigl(t(\mathbf{x})-\tau(\theta)\bigr)
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[
\bigl(T-\tau(\theta)\bigr)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

## Regularity Conditions {#sec-crlb-regularity-conditions}

The Cramér-Rao lower bound requires the following conditions:

1. Differentiation can be interchanged with integration:

   $$
   \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
   \int f(\mathbf{x};\theta)\,d\mathbf{x}
   =
   \int
   \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
   f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
   \,d\mathbf{x}
   $$

2. The score exists:

   $$
   \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
   \ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
   $$

3. The Fisher information is finite and positive:

   $$
   0
   <
   \mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[
   \left(
   \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
   \ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
   \right)^2
   \right]
   <
   \infty
   $$

::: {.callout-warning}
## Parameter-Dependent Support

The interchange condition generally fails when the parameter appears in an indicator function or determines the support of the distribution.

For example, the standard Cramér-Rao lower bound does not apply directly to

$$
X\sim\operatorname{Uniform}(0,\theta)
$$

because the support $(0,\theta)$ depends on $\theta$.
:::

## Bernoulli Example {#sec-crlb-bernoulli-example}

::: {#exm-crlb-bernoulli}
## Estimating a Bernoulli Probability

Let

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\mathcal{Bern}(p)
$$

Find the Cramér-Rao lower bound for the variance of every unbiased estimator of $p$.

Here,

$$
\theta=p
\qquad
\tau(p)=p
\qquad
\tau'(p)=1
$$
:::

### Fisher information {#sec-bernoulli-fisher-information}

For one observation, the probability mass function is

$$
f(x;p)
=
p^x(1-p)^{1-x}
\mathbb{I}_{\{0,1\}}(x)
$$

The joint probability mass function is

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x};p)
&=
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\left[
 p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}
 \mathbb{I}_{\{0,1\}}(x_i)
\right] \\
&=
p^{\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}
(1-p)^{n-\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbb{I}_{\{0,1\}}(x_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the logarithm and omitting the parameter-free indicator term gives

$$
\ln f(\mathbf{x};p)
=
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
\right)\ln p
+
\left(
 n-\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
\right)\ln(1-p)
$$

Differentiating with respect to $p$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial p}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};p)
&=
\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}{p}
-
\frac{n-\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}{1-p} \\
&=
\frac{
(1-p)\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
-p\left(n-\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i\right)
}{p(1-p)} \\
&=
\frac{
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i-np
}{p(1-p)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let

$$
Y=\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
\sim
\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p)
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_n(p)
&=
\mathbb{E}_p\!\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial p}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};p)
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_p\!\left[
\left(
\frac{Y-np}{p(1-p)}
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\frac{1}{p^2(1-p)^2}
\underbrace{
\mathbb{E}_p\!\left[(Y-np)^2\right]
}_{
\displaystyle
\operatorname{Var}_p(Y)
} \\
&=
\frac{np(1-p)}{p^2(1-p)^2} \\
&=
\boxed{
\frac{n}{p(1-p)}
}
\end{aligned}
$$

### The lower bound {#sec-bernoulli-lower-bound}

Since $\tau'(p)=1$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}_p(\widehat{p})
&\geq
\frac{[\tau'(p)]^2}{I_n(p)} \\
&=
\frac{1^2}{n/[p(1-p)]} \\
&=
\boxed{
\frac{p(1-p)}{n}
}
\end{aligned}
$$

### The sample mean attains the bound {#sec-bernoulli-sample-mean}

Consider

$$
\widehat{p}=\overline{X}
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
$$

Its expectation is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_p[\overline{X}]
&=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbb{E}_p[X_i] \\
&=
\frac{1}{n}(np) \\
&=p
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $\overline{X}$ is unbiased. Its variance is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}_p(\overline{X})
&=
\frac{1}{n^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\operatorname{Var}_p(X_i) \\
&=
\frac{1}{n^2}
\bigl[np(1-p)\bigr] \\
&=
\boxed{
\frac{p(1-p)}{n}
}
\end{aligned}
$$

The variance of $\overline{X}$ equals the Cramér-Rao lower bound. Therefore, $\overline{X}$ is an unbiased estimator of $p$ with the smallest possible variance.

::: {#def-umvue}
## Uniformly Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator

Because $\overline{X}$ attains the lower bound for every $p\in(0,1)$, it is a uniformly minimum variance unbiased estimator of $p$.

Uniformly means that its variance is no greater than the variance of any other unbiased estimator at every value of $p$, rather than only at one particular value.
:::


## Reflection
